statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
You need to construct a field for the game "Minesweeper" consisting of $ 2 $ rows and several columns. Each cell must either be empty or contain a mine. We say that two cells are neighboring if they share a side and/or a corner.

The field you are constructing must satisfy the following constraints:

- each empty cell must be neighboring at most one mine;
- the number of empty cells that have a neighboring cell with a mine is equal to $ k $ ;
- among all suitable fields, the number of columns must be the minimum possible.

Construct such a field or report that it is impossible.

Input Format
The first line contains one integer $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 100 $ ) — the number of test cases.

Each test case consists of one line containing one integer $ k $ ( $ 1 \le k \le 100 $ ) — the required number of empty cells which have a neighboring cell with a mine.

Output Format
For each test case, output the answer as follows:

- if it is impossible to construct a field that satisfies all constraints, print NO;
- otherwise, on the first line print YES, and on the second line print an integer $ n $ , where $ n $ is the minimum number of columns in the field. On the third and fourth lines, print the description of the field itself. The description of each row of the field must consist of $ n $ characters, each of which is either a dot "." (empty cell) or an asterisk "\*" (mine). If there are multiple suitable fields, output any of them.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
5
1
4
8
10
9
Output
YES
1
*
.
NO
YES
5
*....
...*.
YES
6
.*..*.
......
NO

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2199C - Minesweeper
Let there be two mines, one in column $$$i$$$ and the other in column $$$j$$$. If $$$|i-j| \le 2$$$ (that is, the mines are in the same column, in adjacent columns, or one column apart), then at least one cell will be adjacent to both of these mines; this means that there must also be a mine in that cell. It can be shown that this will ultimately lead to the entire field being filled with mines. Therefore, in the answer, the column numbers where the mines are located must differ by at least $$$3$$$, and there cannot be two mines in the same column.
If one mine is placed in a column, it will increase the number of free cells adjacent to the mines by $$$5$$$, except when it is in the first or last column (in which case the number of free cells adjacent to the mines will only increase by $$$3$$$). Moreover, no more than two mines can increase the number of these cells by $$$3$$$ (one in the first column and one in the last column). Therefore, for an answer to exist, $$$k$$$ must be expressible as $$$k = 5m$$$, $$$k = 5m + 3$$$, or $$$k = 5m + 6$$$, where $$$m$$$ is a non-negative integer. If such a representation exists for some $$$k$$$, it is unique (due to different remainders modulo $$$5$$$). We will find such a representation, placing $$$m$$$ blocks of $$$3$$$ columns, each containing a mine, and if necessary, we will add $$$2$$$ columns to the left and/or right with a mine in the first/last column.
The case $$$k=1$$$ needs to be considered separately

if

==
0

"NO"

return
}
println
(
"YES"

println

n
)
val a
=
CharArray
(
n
)
{
'.'
}
println
(
a
.
joinToString
(
""
))
for
(
i
in
pos
)
{
a
[
i
]
=
'*'
}
println
(
a
.
joinToString
(
""
))
}

(
1
,
mutableListOf
(
0
))

@repeat

=
0

=
mutableListOf
<
Int
>()

(

%
5
==
1
||
k
%
5
==
3
)

n
+=
2

.

(
0
)

-=
3

>=

3

(

-

%
5
==
3

printAns
(

==
0

0
,
